Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 41: ﻿general info & description Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Customer Company Version: 1.0 Date: 15.10.2025 Author: IT Infrastructure Status: Approved This document describes the standard for the print and scan infrastructure within the customer company. The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles: • Central printer management via print servers • Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO) • Standardized driver versions • Location-based printer concepts • Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail The print infrastructure is based on: • Windows print servers • Location-based printer queues • User- or group-based assignment • VLAN-separated printer networks Manual installation of local printers is not intended. design Architecture overview User device → Print server → Printer (in printer VLAN) Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server. Printer provisioning Printers are: • Created as a queue on the print server • Configured with a standardized driver • Assigned via GPO based on location or department Default printers are set automatically. Driver standard Only approved and tested driver versions are used. After Windows updates, it must be verified: • Whether drivers remain compatible • Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active • Whether printers are still mapped correctly Scan-to-Mail Scan-to-Mail is performed: • Via a dedicated SMTP relay • Using a configured service account • Secured with TLS Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality. Location concept Each location has: • At least one default printer • One multifunction printer with scan capability • Assignment via AD groups Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning? A) Printing affected A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”? • No → Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group. If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied. If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update. If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server. • Yes → Proceed to A2. A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue? • No → Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment). • Yes → Proceed to A3. A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue? • Yes → Check whether the queue is paused. If not → Check the Print Spooler service. If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN). • No → Proceed to A4. A4: Does the issue affect only one location? • Yes → Check VLAN reachability. Check DNS resolution of the printer. Check location-based GPO assignment. • No → Check driver compatibility or print server service. A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update? • Yes → Check driver compatibility. Check Point-and-Print policy. Validate security policies. • No → Further analysis according to standard process. B) Scanning affected B1: Is printing still working? • Yes → Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path. • No → First analyze printing issue according to section A. B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning? • SMTP or connection error → Check SMTP relay reachability. Check service account authentication credentials. Validate TLS configuration. • No error message, but no delivery → Check mail queue. Check spam/filter rules.

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 169: # MFP-BASEL-01 Printer Queue — ACL / AD Group Permission Incident The community centers on a single production printer queue (PRINTER QUEUE (MFP-BASEL-01 QUEUE)) whose queue-level Access Control List (PRINTER ACL) included both a group Allow (PRT-Basel) and an explicit Deny ACE applied to an individual user. The environment's Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard requires that printer access be controlled via AD groups and that NTFS/share permissions be kept consistent; the explicit Deny originating from a prior test violated that model, overrode the group Allow and caused an "Access denied" condition for a user. The explicit Deny was identified as the root cause and was removed during remediation. The records link the printer queue, its ACL composition, the AD group membership, and the broader requirement to align NTFS/share permissions on the print server. ## Root cause: an explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue overrode group Allow permissions The immediate cause of the user's "Access denied" error was an explicit Deny Access Control Entry (ACE) present on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue that blocked the affected user, which took precedence over the group-based Allow entries and prevented printing. The Printer ACL record describes the coexistence of the AD group Allow and the problematic explicit Deny ACE that produced the failure, and the explicit Deny entry is called out as the immediate blocker. This behavior and the specific queue are recorded in the incident notes which identify the queue and the deny ACE as the root cause that was removed during remediation. The evidence ties the queue, the ACL, and the explicit deny entry together as the causal chain for the operational failure. [Data: Entities (71,73); Relationships (119,123)] ## AD group-based permission model was in place (PRT-Basel) but was negated by the deny ACE The MFP-BASEL-01 queue was configured to grant print permission to AD groups (example: PRT-Basel). The affected user was a member of that AD group, which under normal operation would allow printing via the group Allow ACE. However, the explicit Deny ACE applied directly to the user on the queue overrode the group Allow, demonstrating a misalignment between the intended group-based permission model and the effective ACL state. This relationship is documented as the queue granting rights to PRT-Basel while the deny ACE remained present on the same queue. [Data: Entities (71); Relationships (114,119)] ## Standards require group-based access and NTFS/share permission consistency on the print server The Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard specifies that access to print services should be controlled via AD group membership rather than individual ACEs, and that NTFS and share permissions must be consistent for correct print service operation. This standard is explicitly referenced in the filesystem/share permissions entity and was a consideration when reviewing the print server configuration. The relationship between the print server and NTFS/share permission consistency is documented, indicating that the standard is applicable to print server configuration and was relevant to the troubleshooting and remediation of this incident. [Data: Entities (74); Relationships (113)] ## The explicit Deny originated from a prior test and was removed during remediation Records indicate the explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue was introduced by a prior test configuration and remained in place at the time of the incident, causing the access failure for the affected user. The impacted queue record explicitly notes the additional explicit Deny originating from a prior test and that it was removed as part of remediation steps. This confirms the incident was caused by a configuration artifact (test-sourced deny) rather than a systemic AD membership failure, and that removal of the deny entry resolved the immediate access issue. [Data: Entities (71,73)] ## Queue-level permission model failure: individual ACEs at queue-level can invalidate group-based governance The queue-level permissions applied to the MFP-BASEL-01 queue (the ACL and ACE composition) deviated from the standard model by containing an individual explicit Deny ACE. Queue-level misconfiguration is specifically called out as the reason the user's print attempts were blocked, illustrating how local ACEs at the queue override intended group governance. This points to an operational failure mode where ad-hoc or test ACL changes at the queue level cause immediate service denial, reinforcing the need to enforce the group-only ACE model for queues. [Data: Entities (78,73); Relationships (120,119)] ## Printer ACL composition is a repeatable risk pattern deserving audit and monitoring The Printer ACL as an object contains ACEs including both group Allow entries and, in this incident, an explicit Deny for an individual user — a composition that produced a production-impacting access failure. The combination of ACL entries (group allows plus individual denies) is highlighted in the printer ACL record and is central to the incident. Given that an individual deny can negate correct group membership, the ACL composition at queues is a high-risk configuration pattern; without regular audits, these deny entries (whether from tests or troubleshooting) can persist and cause user-impacting outages. This motivates periodic ACL audits and change-detection for printer queues. [Data: Entities (77,73); Relationships (119)] ## Priority remediation and governance actions derived from the incident Immediate action already taken (removal of the explicit Deny) fixed the symptom for the affected user; the incident records show the deny was removed during remediation. Building on that, prioritized follow-up actions should include: (1) an immediate inventory and audit of printer queue ACLs to locate other individual Deny ACEs, (2) enforcement of the standard that queue permissions must be group-based only, (3) update of change-control/runbook procedures so test ACEs cannot persist in production, and (4) alerting or monitoring for direct ACE modifications on print queues so regressions are detected early. These recommendations are grounded in the incident data: the queue-level misconfiguration, the standard's requirement for AD-group control, and the documented removal of the deny ACE. Implementing these steps addresses the exact failure mode observed (individual Deny ACE overriding group Allow) and aligns operations with the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles: • Central printer management via print servers • Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO) • Standardized driver versions • Location-based printer concepts • Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail The print infrastructure is based on: • Windows print servers • Location-based printer queues • User- or group-based assignment • VLAN-separated printer networks Manual installation of local printers is not intended. design Architecture overview User device → Print server → Printer (in printer VLAN) Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server. Printer provisioning Printers are: • Created as a queue on the print server • Configured with a standardized driver • Assigned via GPO based on location or department Default printers are set automatically. Driver standard Only approved and tested driver versions are used. After Windows updates, it must be verified: • Whether drivers remain compatible • Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active • Whether printers are still mapped correctly Scan-to-Mail Scan-to-Mail is performed: • Via a dedicated SMTP relay • Using a configured service account • Secured with TLS Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality. Location concept Each location has: • At least one default printer • One multifunction printer with scan capability • Assignment via AD groups Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning? A) Printing affected A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”? • No → Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group. If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied. If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update. If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server. • Yes → Proceed to A2. A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue? • No → Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment). • Yes → Proceed to A3. A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue? • Yes → Check whether the queue is paused. If not → Check the Print Spooler service. If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN). • No → Proceed to A4. A4: Does the issue affect only one location? • Yes → Check VLAN reachability. Check DNS resolution of the printer. Check location-based GPO assignment. • No → Check driver compatibility or print server service. A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update? • Yes → Check driver compatibility. Check Point-and-Print policy. Validate security policies. • No → Further analysis according to standard process. B) Scanning affected B1: Is printing still working? • Yes → Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path. • No → First analyze printing issue according to section A. B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning? • SMTP or connection error → Check SMTP relay reachability. Check service account authentication credentials. Validate TLS configuration. • No error message, but no delivery → Check mail queue. Check spam/filter rules.

REPORT 169: # MFP-BASEL-01 Printer Queue — ACL / AD Group Permission Incident The community centers on a single production printer queue (PRINTER QUEUE (MFP-BASEL-01 QUEUE)) whose queue-level Access Control List (PRINTER ACL) included both a group Allow (PRT-Basel) and an explicit Deny ACE applied to an individual user. The environment's Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard requires that printer access be controlled via AD groups and that NTFS/share permissions be kept consistent; the explicit Deny originating from a prior test violated that model, overrode the group Allow and caused an "Access denied" condition for a user. The explicit Deny was identified as the root cause and was removed during remediation. The records link the printer queue, its ACL composition, the AD group membership, and the broader requirement to align NTFS/share permissions on the print server. ## Root cause: an explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue overrode group Allow permissions The immediate cause of the user's "Access denied" error was an explicit Deny Access Control Entry (ACE) present on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue that blocked the affected user, which took precedence over the group-based Allow entries and prevented printing. The Printer ACL record describes the coexistence of the AD group Allow and the problematic explicit Deny ACE that produced the failure, and the explicit Deny entry is called out as the immediate blocker. This behavior and the specific queue are recorded in the incident notes which identify the queue and the deny ACE as the root cause that was removed during remediation. The evidence ties the queue, the ACL, and the explicit deny entry together as the causal chain for the operational failure. [Data: Entities (71,73); Relationships (119,123)] ## AD group-based permission model was in place (PRT-Basel) but was negated by the deny ACE The MFP-BASEL-01 queue was configured to grant print permission to AD groups (example: PRT-Basel). The affected user was a member of that AD group, which under normal operation would allow printing via the group Allow ACE. However, the explicit Deny ACE applied directly to the user on the queue overrode the group Allow, demonstrating a misalignment between the intended group-based permission model and the effective ACL state. This relationship is documented as the queue granting rights to PRT-Basel while the deny ACE remained present on the same queue. [Data: Entities (71); Relationships (114,119)] ## Standards require group-based access and NTFS/share permission consistency on the print server The Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard specifies that access to print services should be controlled via AD group membership rather than individual ACEs, and that NTFS and share permissions must be consistent for correct print service operation. This standard is explicitly referenced in the filesystem/share permissions entity and was a consideration when reviewing the print server configuration. The relationship between the print server and NTFS/share permission consistency is documented, indicating that the standard is applicable to print server configuration and was relevant to the troubleshooting and remediation of this incident. [Data: Entities (74); Relationships (113)] ## The explicit Deny originated from a prior test and was removed during remediation Records indicate the explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue was introduced by a prior test configuration and remained in place at the time of the incident, causing the access failure for the affected user. The impacted queue record explicitly notes the additional explicit Deny originating from a prior test and that it was removed as part of remediation steps. This confirms the incident was caused by a configuration artifact (test-sourced deny) rather than a systemic AD membership failure, and that removal of the deny entry resolved the immediate access issue. [Data: Entities (71,73)] ## Queue-level permission model failure: individual ACEs at queue-level can invalidate group-based governance The queue-level permissions applied to the MFP-BASEL-01 queue (the ACL and ACE composition) deviated from the standard model by containing an individual explicit Deny ACE. Queue-level misconfiguration is specifically called out as the reason the user's print attempts were blocked, illustrating how local ACEs at the queue override intended group governance. This points to an operational failure mode where ad-hoc or test ACL changes at the queue level cause immediate service denial, reinforcing the need to enforce the group-only ACE model for queues. [Data: Entities (78,73); Relationships (120,119)] ## Printer ACL composition is a repeatable risk pattern deserving audit and monitoring The Printer ACL as an object contains ACEs including both group Allow entries and, in this incident, an explicit Deny for an individual user — a composition that produced a production-impacting access failure. The combination of ACL entries (group allows plus individual denies) is highlighted in the printer ACL record and is central to the incident. Given that an individual deny can negate correct group membership, the ACL composition at queues is a high-risk configuration pattern; without regular audits, these deny entries (whether from tests or troubleshooting) can persist and cause user-impacting outages. This motivates periodic ACL audits and change-detection for printer queues. [Data: Entities (77,73); Relationships (119)] ## Priority remediation and governance actions derived from the incident Immediate action already taken (removal of the explicit Deny) fixed the symptom for the affected user; the incident records show the deny was removed during remediation. Building on that, prioritized follow-up actions should include: (1) an immediate inventory and audit of printer queue ACLs to locate other individual Deny ACEs, (2) enforcement of the standard that queue permissions must be group-based only, (3) update of change-control/runbook procedures so test ACEs cannot persist in production, and (4) alerting or monitoring for direct ACE modifications on print queues so regressions are detected early. These recommendations are grounded in the incident data: the queue-level misconfiguration, the standard's requirement for AD-group control, and the documented removal of the deny ACE. Implementing these steps addresses the exact failure mode observed (individual Deny ACE overriding group Allow) and aligns operations with the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. [Data: Entities (71,73,74,78,77); Relationships (120,119,114)]

Function restored. Activities 06.11.25 08:25 – Email from Contact Person 15 Good morning Since yesterday, the printer “MFP-Basel-01” is no longer responding to print jobs. The documents remain in the queue, and in some cases disappear without being printed. Other printers in the building are functioning normally. Could you please check this? Kind regards 06.11.25 08:37 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 21. 06.11.25 09:10 – Initial analysis Remote access to an affected user. Print job briefly appears in the print server queue, then disappears without printing. Queue itself is not paused. Print Spooler service is running. Test print directly from the print server to the printer also fails. 06.11.25 10:05 – Inquiry to customer Good day Were there any recent changes to the network or infrastructure at the Basel location? Thank you for your brief feedback. 06.11.25 11:20 – Customer response Last week, a new network segment for devices was introduced. The printers were moved into a separate VLAN. No other changes are known to us. 07.11.25 09:00 – Further analysis Ping from print server to printer unsuccessful. Ping from a client within the same location network successful. This indicates segmentation between the server network and the printer VLAN. Comparison with document “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Version 1.0”. In the section Location Concept, it is defined that printers operate in a separate VLAN but must be reachable from the print server. Routing and firewall rules must be configured accordingly. 07.11.25 11:30 – Internal coordination with Network Team Confirmation that the new firewall rule did not allow the server network access to the printer VLAN. Rule adjusted according to standard. 10.11.25 – Test phase Printing from print server successful. Multiple test prints from user workstations performed. No abnormalities observed. 12.11.25 14:40 – Customer feedback Printer is functioning normally again. Larger print jobs are also processed correctly. 12.11.25 14:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 15 The root cause was a missing network allowance between the print server and the newly configured printer VLAN at the Basel location. The configuration was adjusted according to the “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard”. The printer is fully reachable again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 41: ﻿general info & description Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Customer Company Version: 1.0 Date: 15.10.2025 Author: IT Infrastructure Status: Approved This document describes the standard for the print and scan infrastructure within the customer company. The objective is a stable, centrally managed printing environment for all locations based on the following core principles: • Central printer management via print servers • Automatic printer assignment via Group Policy (GPO) • Standardized driver versions • Location-based printer concepts • Uniform configuration for Scan-to-Mail The print infrastructure is based on: • Windows print servers • Location-based printer queues • User- or group-based assignment • VLAN-separated printer networks Manual installation of local printers is not intended. design Architecture overview User device → Print server → Printer (in printer VLAN) Printers are not directly accessible from the client but are addressed exclusively via the central print server. Printer provisioning Printers are: • Created as a queue on the print server • Configured with a standardized driver • Assigned via GPO based on location or department Default printers are set automatically. Driver standard Only approved and tested driver versions are used. After Windows updates, it must be verified: • Whether drivers remain compatible • Whether Point-and-Print restrictions are active • Whether printers are still mapped correctly Scan-to-Mail Scan-to-Mail is performed: • Via a dedicated SMTP relay • Using a configured service account • Secured with TLS Changes to mail servers or authentication mechanisms may affect scan functionality. Location concept Each location has: • At least one default printer • One multifunction printer with scan capability • Assignment via AD groups Printers are located in a separate printer VLAN. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue occur when printing or when scanning? A) Printing affected A1: Is the printer visible in Windows Device Manager or in “Printers & Scanners”? • No → Check whether the user is a member of the correct AD group. If yes → Check whether the GPO has been applied. If GPO not applied → Perform Group Policy update. If GPO applied → Check reachability of the print server. • Yes → Proceed to A2. A2: Is the print job displayed in the queue? • No → Issue is likely client-side (check driver or assignment). • Yes → Proceed to A3. A3: Does the print job remain stuck in the queue? • Yes → Check whether the queue is paused. If not → Check the Print Spooler service. If Spooler is running → Check network connection to the printer (Ping/VLAN). • No → Proceed to A4. A4: Does the issue affect only one location? • Yes → Check VLAN reachability. Check DNS resolution of the printer. Check location-based GPO assignment. • No → Check driver compatibility or print server service. A5: Did the issue occur after a Windows update? • Yes → Check driver compatibility. Check Point-and-Print policy. Validate security policies. • No → Further analysis according to standard process. B) Scanning affected B1: Is printing still working? • Yes → Issue likely in Scan-to-Mail or destination path. • No → First analyze printing issue according to section A. B2: Is an error message displayed during scanning? • SMTP or connection error → Check SMTP relay reachability. Check service account authentication credentials. Validate TLS configuration. • No error message, but no delivery → Check mail queue. Check spam/filter rules.
